I Fundamental Requirements ............................................................................................................................. 58 18 Related Documents ................................................................................................................................................. 58 SEMI E132-1225 SEMI 2004, 2025 semr 1 Purpose 1.1 This Standard specifies a method for restricting access to communication with equipment by requiring clients to authenticate to the equipment before proceeding with subsequent communication, and provides a flexible authorization scheme to control client access. Authorization allows parties who want to exchange information with or control the equipment to do so on a need-to-know and/or need-to-use basis. 1.2 The authorization scheme specified in this Standard allows equipment vendors the flexibility to provide access control at any level of granularity. ranging from no access control restrictions, predefined role-based access control. to very fine-grained control. 2 Scope 2.1 This Standard applies to manufacturing equipment that requires authentication and authorization for its services. It does not apply to communication that is governed by the SEMI EJ0 communication and control state models. 2.2 This Standard does not require data transmitted over an established session to be encrypted, encryption is only required as specified by the authentication protocol. NOTICE: SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. paragraphs.context: in this Standard. 5.2.24 Public Key Cryptography (PKC)-also known as Asymmetric Key Cryptography, a cryptographic algorithm that employs two keys for encryption and decryption of data. One key is designated as the public key and can be freely advertised, while the other key, designated as the private key, is never revealed. Data encrypted with one key can only be decrypted with the other key. PKC is widely used for key exchange and message signing. 5.2.25 Secret Key Cryptography (SKC) - also known as Symmetric Key Cryptography, a cryptographic algorithm that employs one single key for both encryption and decryption. SKC is widely used for bulk encryption of data due to its speed over PKC. 5.2.26 secure communications - communication between two entities where the content is protected using encryption. 5.2.27 Security Adm in Agent -a Client that manages session access and privileges on the Equipment Server. 5.2.28 session - refers to an Authenticated Session unless specified otherwise. Fig-urc S Abstract Model of ACL I 0.2.1 Access control privileges are assigned to defined clients (principals) by using an Access Control List (ACL). An ACL is a collection of ACL entries, each entry granting a subset of the available privileges to a specific subject. A subject can represent a specific principal. a uniquely-named role, or all principals that successfully authenticate with the equipment. A principal can have privileges granted to it through either an explicit list of privileges, or indirectly through assignment to a role. The equipment shall maintain the ACL defined in this Specification in nonvolatile memory. I 0.2.2 Roles are used to simplify the management of privilege assignments to principals. Each unique role has an ACL entry that specifies the privileges assigned to that role. Those privileges can then be granted to principals through a RoleAssignment ACL entry for each principal that should have those privileges. A principal shall either have a RoleAssignment or an explicit list of privileges assigned to it (PrivilegeAssignment); it is not permitted to have both. I 0.2.3 The equipment shall refuse session requests from clients that have no ACLEntry, either explicitly through a role or privilege assignment or implicitly through the existence of an ACLEntry for all authenticated principals. If there are no such entries for the authenticating client, the equipment shall reject the session request. If there is a PrivilegeAssignment or RoleAssignment ACLEntry for both the specific Principal and the reserved 'anyPrincipal'. the equipment shall accept the session request, and shall grant only those privileges assigned to the specific Principal to the session. Privileges assigned to the reserved 'anyPrincipal' shall not be granted to such sessions. 10.2.8.8 A session established using the PrivilegeAssignment or RoleAssignment ACLEntry for the reserved principal 'urn:serni-org:auth:anyPrincipal' is called a guest session. paragraphs.context: these subjects. 6.1.2 This Specification follows the guidelines for XML as outlined in SEMI E 121. 6.1.3 Some of the key guidelines followed in this Specification are summarized here: Attributes of UML classes are generally represented as XML attributes, to improve efficiency in transferring metadata descriptions from the equipment. Exceptions to this convention are UML attributes that correspond to data structures or other constructs that cannot be represented as XML attributes. or may include text values that may need to include non-parsed character data (CDA TA). Composition associations are mapped to a single XML element (or multiple such elements if the specified multiplicity is greater than one). Aggregations are modeled as contained elements or arrays of references to one or more unique attributes of the target type. Inheritance is modeled using complexType extension. In cases where a reference to an abstract base class occurs in the UML model, a choice compositor for each of the possible derived types is used in order to restrict instance documents from including arbitrary extension through type substitution. 6.1.4 The translation of a UML class to XML Schema types is documented using a table format illustrated by Table I. Operations defined for a UML class are translated into WSDL operations. 6.1.5 Translation Table Column Header Description Atrribute or Role Name - If an attribute, the name of the attribute is placed here. This Specification defines several restrictions on the capabilities of SSL to the minimum necessary to support SEMI E 132 authentication. These restrictions are described in more detail in the following section . 7 .1.3 SEMI E 132 Restrictions on SSL 7.1 .3.1 X.509 v3 public key certificates as defined by RFC 2459 must be used to establish equipment and client identities, refer to X.509 documentation referenced in 4.6 for complete details o f X.509 structure and content. The certificate is sent when required during the SSL handshake. 7.1.3.2 Mutual authentication is required. Equipment shall always send its certificate and request for the client's certificate as part of the SSL h andshake. [/RQ] 8.3.2.3 For security reasons, some equipment users may prefer to only support the scenario where EDA Applications connect to the Equipment Server and not allow the Equipment Server to open connections to other machines. (They do not want to expose a gRPC port on EDA Application machines and they do not want to open up ports on the firewall as it increases risk.) In this situation, the Equipment Server is prevented from opening connections to other machines. 8.3.2.3.1 Similarly, some equipment users may prefer to only allow Equipment Server to open connection to other machines using secure communications. This Related Information was approved for publication by full letter ballot procedures on September 7, 2021. R1-1 Background Information RI -I.I EDA Freeze Version 2 used HTTP/I. I with SOAP/X ML as the mechanism to transmit information between EDA clients and EDA servers. This section describes bow that functionality can be mapped to gRPC and Protocol Buffers technology. R 1-1.2 It is not as efficient to structure Protocol Buffers messages exactly the same as the original SOAP/XML messages defined in SEMI E 132.1. It does not leverage the strengths of the new technology (e.g., oneof fields) and there are workarounds put into the X ML schema to deal with the string representation limitations within SOAP/XML that no longer make sense (e.g., sending additional metadata and additional layers of infonnation). R1-2 Message Structures R 1-2.1 With SEMI E 132.1, Sessionld, Client, and Equipment information were encoded in the SOAP Header via the SyncHeader and SyncHashHeader information per SEMI E 128. (SyncHeader has From. To, and Sessionld elements. SyncHashHeader bas From, To, and SessionldHasbed elements.) This meant that every message sent to and from the EDA server had access to this information. gRPC and Protocol Buffers do not use SOAP Headers so a new infrastructure needs to be used. The Equipmentld information and Session1d information will be specified for gRPC methods via the gRPC metadata infrastructure. The Clientld information will be specified for specific gRPC

R1-3 Hashing Session Identifiers R l -3.1 SEMI El28 specified the SessionldHashed that should use the SHA-I hash function. This hash function has been deprecated. and this Specification uses SHA-256 as defined in the Secure Hash Standard (SHS). SHA-256 is part of the SHA-2 family of hashing algorithms. Per NIST's Policy on Hash Functions (August 2015), SHA-256 is the minimum recommended for interoperability and there is no need to transition to SHA-3 family of hash algorithms (also defined in the SHS). Since EDA software using gRPC/Protocol Buffers will never be talking to SOAP/XML implementations, there is no concern for backwards compatibility. Protocol Buffers do not have built-in support for a data type to store UUID data, so the Sessionld will be stored in a string data type. Rl -5.2 With Protocol Buffers messages, error information is always specified in the Protocol Buffers message as an oneof structure. If the operation was successful, the implementer should not assign anything to the Errorlnformation structure. Protocol Buffers syntax does not allow oneof structures to be embedded in another oneof structure. All the fields in an oneofstructure share the same memory, and at most one field can be set at the same time. Setting any member of the oneof fields automatically clears all the other members. You can check which value in a oneoffield is specified (if any) using a special case() or WbichOneof() method, depending on the target development language. If there is nothing assigned to the Errorlnformation field, the case() or WbichOneOf(} method specifies None. This can be used to detect if the operation was successful. If the operation was not successful (something is assigned to the Errorlnformation field), the other message parameters that normally contain data will not be valid (for example, ResultsData field). Rl -5.3 All methods that return information to the caller should have Errorlnformation field defined in the response message. This allows the Errorlnformation field to be extended if additional error structures are required in the future, and backward compatibility issues are minimized. R1-6 Session Ping R 1 -6. 1 SessionPing was originally used to ensure the remote entity is still active. With HTTP/ 1 . 1, the other side could disappear and the sending entity would never know. With gRPC and bidirectional streams, each side of the connection can know if the connection is closed and the implementing software can take action accordingly. R 1 -6.2 Session Ping functionality will still be left in place to allow the Equipment Server to check that the EDA software on the other side is still responding. It is possible for the TCP/IP connection to be active, but the software application above that layer is not responding. Rl -6.3 A new mechanism (lsEquipmentServerAlive) is used to allow the Client to check if the Equipment Server is responding. Having the mechanism called SessionPing for both directions was confusing when diagnosing Equipment Server issues. With respect to the EDA Standards, it includes, but is not limited to: Awhemication - Proving an application or system is who it claims to be. Awhori=ation - Preventing applications and systems from using resources they are not allowed to. Confidentiality- Protecrjng sensitive information from unauthorized disclosure. Data Integrity- Detecting if there is unauthorized modification of data. R2-l.2 Many of the security definitions and explanations that were part of SEMI El32.I do not need to be included in this Specification. For convenience, high-level security concepts are provided in this Related lnfonnation. For more specific details. please refer to the appropriate documentation. R2-2 Public Key Certificates R2-2. l A Public Key Infrastructure (PK.I) is a system of facilities, policies, and services that supports the use of public key cryptography for authenticating the parties involved in a transaction. The X.509 standards provide the basis for the industry standard Public Key Infrastructure. R2-2.2 Public key certificates are managed by the equipment users (factory). They should decide what is an acceptable level of security that will address their needs, and set up the certificate generation, distribution, and renewal process to

paragraphs.context: The X.509 standards provide the basis for the industry standard Public Key Infrastructure. R2-2.2 Public key certificates are managed by the equipment users (factory). They should decide what is an acceptable level of security that will address their needs, and set up the certificate generation, distribution, and renewal process to satisfy that level of security. They are also responsible for ensuring the certificates used by the equipment and software applications that coMect to it are compatible. (If the certificates are not compatible, the software will not be able to establish a secure connection.) R2-2.3 Certificates are digjtally signed by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA). The authenticity of the certificate is verified by the CA signature within the certificate. (In effect. the CA is certifying the validity of the information contained in the certificate, and certifies the binding between the entity identified in the certificate and its public key used for authentication purposes in SSUfLS.) R2-2.4 For communication over the lntemet, it is desirable to get a certificate signed by a publicly recognized and trusted CA to ensure the chain of trust. However, for internal factory networks, it might not be necessary or cost effective to get certificates used by the equipment and software applications digitally signed in such a manner. ln this case, a factory may choose to be their own CA. I The session identifier (SessionJd) is defined in SEMI El 32 and is generated by the e quipment during the SEMI E132 EstablishSession operation. It is a UUID that is guaranteed unique across space and time (refer to RFC 4122 for specific details on syntax and format of a UUID). 9.8.2 In Protocol Buffers messages, the Sessionld is represented as a string data type, as it is more easily understood in its human-readable form. 9.8.3 The session identifier is specified in all requests to the Equipment Server over a SEMI E 132 authenticated session. gRPC's metadata infrastructure is used to pass the Sessionld for gRPC methods.

There is no guarantee of message delivery. 9.12.2 SEMI El32 provides SessionPing functionality that can be used to deterrnine if the operational message recipient is available and responding to messages. It defines a retry sequence with a configurable maximum number of concurrent SessionPing failures before closing the session. 9.12.3 gRPC is a connection-oriented protocol. so it is possible to quickly tell if a message is not delivered. Some equipment users may want SEMI E l 34 NewData delivery errors to be treated as SEMI El32 communication errors, rather than waiting until the SessionPing work:flow detennines the operational message recipient is not responding and closes the session. For example, they do not want the Equipment Server to continue to collect data that cannot be delivered. 9.12.3.1 This behavior is controlled by a configuration switch defined in SEMI E l32.2. 9.12.4 Errors that the Equipment Server could encounter while delivering SEMI E 134 ewData messages include: gRPC errors when sending the NewData message in the streaming gRPC method. This Related Information was approved for publication by full letter ballot procedures on September 7, 2021 . R1-1 Background Information RI-I. I EDA Freeze Version 2 used HTTP/I. I with SOAP/XML as the mechanism to transmit information between EDA clients and EDA servers. This section describes how that functionality can be mapped to gRPC and Protocol Buffers technology. R 1 -1 .2 It is not as efficient to structure Protocol Buffers messages exactly the same as the original SOAP/XML messages defined in SEMJ El34.I. It does not leverage the strengths of the new technology (e.g., oneof fields) and there are workarounds put into the XML schema to deal with the string representation limitations within SOAP/XML that no longer make sense (e.g., sending additional metadata and additional layers of infonnation). R1-2 Message Structures Rl-2.1 With SEMI E 134.1, Sessionid, Client, and Equipment information were encoded in the SOAP Header via the SyncHeader and SyncHashHeader information per SEMJ E 128. paragraphs.context: Structures Rl-2.1 With SEMI E 134.1, Sessionid, Client, and Equipment information were encoded in the SOAP Header via the SyncHeader and SyncHashHeader information per SEMJ E 128. (SyncHeader has From. To, and Sessionid elements. SyncHashHeader has From, To, and SessionldHashed elements.) This meant that every message sent to and from the EDA server had access to this information. gRPC and Protocol Buffers do not use SOAP Headers so a new infrastructure needs to be used. Equipment information and Clientld in the headers was never used and has been removed in this Specification. The Sessionid information will be specified for gRPC methods via the gRPC metadata infrastructure. R 1-2.2 During development of this Standard, DCP Data messages for Event Reports, Exception Reports, and Trace Reports were consolidated into one Protocol Buffers message as they had common elements. Testing showed this approach had a negligible improvement on performance. The Standard reverted back to having dedicated Protocol Buffers messages for each report type to make it easier for implementers (i.e., protobuf message definitions only has information for that report type). Rl -2.3 The ReportType message is used to represent a DataCollectionReportType, which can have multiple Report Types. They will be ordered according to the time of their occurrence, so the index field was removed from this message. R1-3 Wrapper Messages R 1-3.1 For efficiencies, wrapper messages with just a single piece of information were removed when creating equivalent Protocol Buffers messages. The parent message will include that piece of information directly. For example:

R1-3 Wrapper Messages R 1-3.1 For efficiencies, wrapper messages with just a single piece of information were removed when creating equivalent Protocol Buffers messages. The parent message will include that piece of information directly. For example: In SEMI E 134.1 there was a NoSuchPlanType object defined that had a single attribute 'planID' that represented the PlanlD that did not exist. There is no corresponding Protocol Buffers message for NoSuchPlanType. The parent message (GetPlanDefinitionErrorType) will directly reference the PlanlD that did not exist. In SEMI E 138. PVSet was defined to represent a set of data values that can be included in a data collection report. With Protocol Buffers, the data collection report defines a collection of value types for the set of data values directly, bypassing the wrapper class. Rl-3.2 The exception to this approach is the input and output stream parameter messages used in gRPC method calls. gRPC streaming methods will have both a request stream message and response stream message parameter defined (even if they are empty messages), as this will future-proof the method definition. If the RPC method needs to have input parameters in the future, they can be added to the input stream message, rather than redefining the RPC method. SEMI E134.2-1225 SEMI 2022, 2025 semr R1-4 Error Handling R 1 -4.1 Many XML method calls that rerumed information to the calling code had a choice between the service result (if the method call was successful) and an Error result (if the method call was not successful). This was handled by an xsd:choice in the schema file. Method results could have multiple elements. This makes using Protocol Buffers' oneof strucrure difficult. Rl-4.2 With Protocol Buffers messages, error information is always specified in the Protocol Buffers message as an oneof structure. If the operation was successful, the implementer should not assign anything to the Errorlnfonnation strucrure. Protocol Buffers syntax does not allow oneof structures to be embedded in another oneof structure. All the fields in an oneof structure share the same memory, and at most one field can be set at the same time. o Setting any member of the oneof fields automatically clears all the other members. You can check which value in an oneof field is specified (if any) using a special case() or WbichOneof() method, depending on the target development language. o If there is nothing assigned to the Errorlnfonnation field. the case() or WhichOneOf() method will specify 1one. This can be used to detect if the operation was successful. o If the operation was not successful (something is assigned to the Error field), the other message parameters that normally

This can be used to detect if the operation was successful. o If the operation was not successful (something is assigned to the Error field), the other message parameters that normally contain data will not be valid (e.g., ResultsData field). R 1-4.3 All methods that return information to the caller should have Error information defined in the response message. This allows the Error information to be extended if additional errors are required in the future, and backward compatibility issues are minimized. Rl -4.4 Ln the lnvalidTraceRequestType message, there are fields that can be specified only once (invalid interval, invalid cycle, invalid group size). To enforce the 0 .. 1 relationship, the Protocol Buffers oneof structure is used and the calling code can determine if the oneof field is specified to know if the attribute is invalid. (For example, if the interval is invalid, the implementing code should assign a value to Lnvalidlnterval field as appropriate. If the field value is not set. the calling code knows that the interval information is valid.) Refer to SEMI E 132 for more details. R2-2 Collecting Data over Secure Communications R2-2. l The software application who initiates the gRPC connection can specify if that connection is using secure communications. (Refer to SEMI E 132.2.) For example: When the Client establishes a gRPC connection to the Equipment Server, they connect on a different port for secure communications. As part of the SEMI El 32 EstablishSession, if the endpoint information is provided to have the Equipment Server open the connection to the Consumer, the equipment user specifies if it is a secure connection. R2-2.2 An equipment user who wishes to protect the collected data can activate the DCP for a session using secure communications with the operational message recipient. R2-2.2 An equipment user who wishes to protect the collected data can activate the DCP for a session using secure communications with the operational message recipient. R2-2.3 There can be a perfonnance cost to using secure communications, so the equipment user may not wish to send all their data over secure communications. The equipment user would create two sessions, one using secure communications and a second one using not secure communications. For example: The equipment user establishes Session A I to use secure communications, and Session 8 I to use not secure communications. The equipment user wants collected data for DCP A to be sent over secure communications, so they activate DCP A for Session A I. The equipment user does not need collected data for DCP 8 to be sent securely (e.g .. it collects motor temperature data at a high frequency). They activate DCP 8 for Session 8 I. The operational message recipient implementation would have two instances of the streaming gRPC method to handle the collected data (one for Session Al and one for Session Bl). The gRPC infrastructure ensures data sent over the secure communications connection are encrypted correctly. R2-3 Allocating Different Computer Resources to Streaming gRPC Method Calls R2-3. l Each authenticated session has a streaming gRPC call associated with it to handle the collected data for DCPs activated for that session. R2-3.2 gRPC allows multiple calls to the same gRPC method to be executing in parallel. EDA implementers may choose to take advantage of this capability to treat certain data collection plans differently in their software applications (e.g., allocate more computer resources. process it differently, etc.). Having all related data from DCPs associated with the same session in one g.RPC streaming method supports this behavior. For example: An equipment user may choose to have DCPs collecting critical information associated with Session CI, and DCPs for less urgent data associated with Session DI.
